interactive.prompt: Some people prefer to work from home. What do you think are the advantages and disadvantages of remote work?
interactive.followUp: You mentioned some challenges of remote work. Describe a specific strategy a company could use to solve one of them, and explain why it would help.
interactive.model: Working from home has clear benefits as well as real drawbacks. On the positive side, remote workers save the time and money they would normally spend commuting, and they can often arrange their day around the hours when they concentrate best. Many people also report that they eat better and exercise more when they control their own schedule. However, there are disadvantages too. Employees can feel isolated when they rarely see their colleagues face to face, and new staff in particular may struggle to learn the unwritten rules of a company through a screen. In addition, the boundary between work and private life can disappear, so some people actually work longer hours at home than they did in the office. Overall, I believe remote work suits experienced employees best, while newer workers benefit from at least some time in a shared office.
interactive.modelFollowUp: One practical strategy is a fixed weekly team day, when everyone comes to the office on the same date. For example, a software company could require all developers to attend on Wednesdays for planning meetings, training, and shared lunches. This directly attacks the isolation problem because people build relationships in person, and junior staff can ask quick questions naturally. At the same time, employees keep the flexibility of remote work for the other four days.

interactive.prompt: Many parents give smartphones to young children. Do you think children under twelve should have their own smartphones? Why or why not?
interactive.followUp: Some people say the problem is not the phone itself but how it is used. What specific rules would you set for a ten-year-old with a phone, and why?
interactive.model: In my opinion, children under twelve generally should not have their own smartphones, although I understand why parents give them. The main argument in favor is safety: a phone lets a child call home in an emergency and lets parents check where their child is. However, a modern smartphone is far more than a communication tool. It gives young children unlimited access to social media, advertising, and games designed to be addictive, at an age when they cannot yet control their impulses. Studies also link heavy phone use with poor sleep and shorter attention spans. A better solution is a simple phone that only makes calls and sends messages, which provides the safety benefits without the risks. Once children are older and have learned digital habits at school and at home, a full smartphone becomes a more reasonable gift.
interactive.modelFollowUp: I would set three concrete rules. First, no phone in the bedroom after eight in the evening, because late screen use clearly damages children's sleep. Second, social media accounts would not be allowed at all, since ten-year-olds cannot handle the social pressure and strangers online. Third, I would limit entertainment apps to one hour a day using parental controls. These rules keep the useful parts of the phone while blocking the most harmful ones.

interactive.prompt: Some people love living in big cities, while others prefer small towns. Which do you prefer, and what are the main reasons for your choice?
interactive.followUp: Think of one specific thing from the option you did NOT choose that you would miss. How would you deal with missing it?
interactive.model: I prefer living in a big city, mainly because of opportunity and variety. Cities concentrate jobs, universities, hospitals, and cultural life in one place, so a young person can change careers, meet new people, or study something unusual without moving house. In my experience, the energy of a city is also motivating: when everyone around you is building something, you want to build something too. Of course, city life has costs. Rent is high, the air is often polluted, and people can be surprisingly lonely in a crowd. Small towns offer cleaner air, lower prices, and a strong sense of community, and I respect people who choose them. However, for the stage of life I am in now, the professional and social possibilities of a large city clearly outweigh its problems, so the city remains my choice.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The thing I would miss most from small-town life is the quiet, especially at night, because constant traffic noise genuinely wears me down. To deal with this, I would choose an apartment on a side street rather than a main road, even if it meant a longer walk to the metro. I would also make a habit of leaving the city one weekend each month, hiking somewhere silent to reset before returning.

interactive.prompt: Online courses are becoming very popular. Do you think learning online is as effective as learning in a traditional classroom?
interactive.followUp: Describe one subject or skill that you think is almost impossible to learn well online, and explain what makes it so difficult.
interactive.model: I believe online learning can be effective, but only for certain subjects and certain kinds of students. For theoretical material such as programming, history, or grammar, online courses are excellent: students can pause a video, repeat a difficult section, and study at midnight if that suits them. The best online platforms also give instant feedback through quizzes, which a busy classroom teacher cannot always provide. However, online learning demands strong self-discipline, and completion rates for internet courses are famously low. A classroom creates commitment: the fixed timetable, the teacher watching you, and the classmates who notice when you are absent all push students to continue. Face-to-face classes are also better for discussion, teamwork, and hands-on practice. In short, online learning is a powerful tool for motivated students studying theory, but it has not replaced the classroom, and I doubt it ever fully will.
interactive.modelFollowUp: I think it is almost impossible to learn to swim online. The skill is entirely physical: your success depends on how your body feels in the water, and no video can tell you that your legs are sinking or your breathing rhythm is wrong at the exact moment it happens. A coach standing at the pool edge corrects these errors in real time. Without that immediate physical feedback, most learners simply practice their mistakes.

interactive.prompt: Some people say that traveling to other countries is an important part of a good education. Do you agree or disagree?
interactive.followUp: Not everyone can afford international travel. Suggest one realistic way a student with little money could gain similar benefits, and explain how it works.
interactive.model: I strongly agree that international travel is a valuable part of education, though I would not call it essential. Books can describe another culture, but standing inside it teaches lessons no classroom can. When travelers order food in a language they barely speak or navigate an unfamiliar train system, they build confidence and problem-solving skills that transfer to every part of life. Travel also destroys stereotypes: it is much harder to believe simple negative ideas about a country after its people have shown you kindness. Perhaps most importantly, seeing how differently other societies handle food, family, and work makes students question habits at home that they had assumed were universal. That said, travel is expensive, and a curious person with a library card can still become educated without a passport. Travel accelerates education powerfully, but it is a catalyst rather than a requirement.
interactive.modelFollowUp: A realistic alternative is volunteering with international students at a local university. Many universities run conversation-partner programs that pair local volunteers with newly arrived students who want language practice. The local student gets hours of genuine cultural exchange every week, hears firsthand how daily life works in another country, and often gains lasting friendships, all without spending money. It is not identical to travel, but it delivers the same core benefit: sustained personal contact with a different worldview.

interactive.prompt: Should governments spend more money on public transportation instead of building new roads for cars? Explain your opinion.
interactive.followUp: Imagine your city doubled its bus service tomorrow but most people still drove cars. What specific measure would convince drivers to switch, and why would it work?
interactive.model: In my view, governments should clearly prioritize public transportation over new roads. Decades of experience show that building extra highways rarely reduces traffic for long, because easier driving simply invites more cars, a pattern known as induced demand. Public transport, by contrast, moves far more people using far less space and energy. A single train can replace hundreds of cars, cutting both congestion and air pollution at the same time. There is also a fairness argument: children, elderly people, and low-income workers who cannot drive depend on buses and trains, so investing in them serves the whole population rather than only car owners. I accept that rural areas still need decent roads, and maintenance of existing highways should continue. But when a government must choose where new money goes, expanding reliable, frequent public transport delivers greater economic, environmental, and social returns than another lane of asphalt.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The most effective measure is making buses genuinely faster than cars by giving them dedicated lanes on major roads. Frequency alone does not win drivers over; speed does. When a commuter sitting in traffic watches a bus glide past in its own lane, the daily comparison becomes painful and personal. Cities like Bogota proved this works at scale. Combined with a simple tap-to-pay fare system, the time advantage converts drivers better than any advertising campaign.

interactive.prompt: Some people believe social media brings friends closer together, while others think it weakens real friendships. What is your opinion?
interactive.followUp: Describe one personal rule you follow, or would like to follow, for using social media in a healthier way, and explain what effect it has.
interactive.model: I believe social media both strengthens and weakens friendships, and the final result depends on how deliberately people use it. On the positive side, it keeps long-distance relationships alive in a way earlier generations could not manage. I can watch a childhood friend's life unfold on another continent and pick up our conversation instantly, which letters and expensive phone calls never allowed. However, social media also tempts us to replace real contact with its cheap imitation. Liking a photo feels like maintaining a friendship, but it involves no listening, no vulnerability, and no shared time, which are the ingredients real closeness requires. Worse, the polished highlights people post can breed quiet envy between friends. My conclusion is that social media is an excellent bridge between meetings but a poor substitute for them. Friends who treat it as a scheduling tool grow closer; those who treat it as the friendship itself drift apart.
interactive.modelFollowUp: My rule is that meaningful news deserves a voice, not a post. When something important happens to a close friend, I call instead of commenting, even if a comment would take ten seconds and a call takes twenty minutes. The effect has been noticeable: those conversations wander into topics a comment thread never reaches, and several friendships that were fading came back to life simply because we started hearing each other again.

interactive.prompt: In some countries, university education is free for all students. Do you think university should be free, or should students pay tuition fees?
interactive.followUp: If your country made university free tomorrow, what is one problem that might appear within five years, and how could it be prevented?
interactive.model: I support free or heavily subsidized university education, though I recognize it involves real trade-offs. The strongest argument is fairness: talent is spread across all income levels, but tuition fees filter students by their parents' wealth rather than by ability. A society that prices bright people out of education wastes its own future doctors and engineers. Free university also frees graduates from debt, letting them choose careers by passion and social value rather than by salary alone; more of them can become teachers, researchers, or nurses. Critics argue that free tuition forces taxpayers, including those who never attended university, to fund other people's degrees. This is true, but graduates repay society through higher lifetime taxes and the skilled work every economy depends on. To keep the system sustainable, entry can remain competitive and based on merit. Education financed collectively is an investment, not a gift.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The likely problem is overcrowding: when the price falls to zero, applications surge, and lecture halls, laboratories, and professors cannot expand as quickly. Within five years, quality could drop for everyone. The prevention is to phase the policy in gradually, starting with fields where the country has worker shortages, while simultaneously funding new teaching staff and buildings. Keeping entrance exams selective during the transition also ensures that places go to prepared students while capacity catches up.

interactive.prompt: Some people enjoy cooking and eating at home, while others prefer eating at restaurants. Which do you prefer, and why?
interactive.followUp: Describe one dish you can cook, or would love to learn, and explain what makes it special to you.
interactive.model: I prefer cooking and eating at home, although I enjoy restaurants for special occasions. The first reason is control: at home I decide exactly how much salt, oil, and sugar goes into my food, which matters more to me every year. The second reason is cost, since a single restaurant dinner in my city equals the grocery budget for several days of home meals. But the deepest reason is that cooking itself has become a form of rest for me. After a day of screens and abstract problems, chopping vegetables and watching onions slowly turn golden is a physical, sensory activity with an immediate reward. Meals at home are also more social in a quiet way; friends who help stir a pot talk more honestly than friends sitting in a loud dining room. Restaurants offer novelty and freedom from washing dishes, but home cooking feeds more than hunger.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The dish I am proudest of is my grandmother's lentil soup. Technically it is simple, just lentils, carrots, cumin, and lemon, but the special part is timing: she taught me to add the lemon only after the heat is off, so the brightness survives. Cooking it takes me straight back to her small kitchen, and now that she is gone, making the soup feels like a conversation with her. Food can store memories better than photographs.

interactive.prompt: How important is it for people to have hobbies outside of work or study? Explain your view with reasons.
interactive.followUp: Some people say they are simply too busy for hobbies. What would you say to convince such a person, using a concrete example?
interactive.model: I think hobbies are far more important than most people admit, and I would rank them close to sleep and exercise as pillars of a healthy life. First, hobbies protect mental health. Work and study constantly measure us against goals and deadlines, while a hobby is a rare space where effort matters more than results; nobody grades your garden or your guitar. Second, hobbies often build skills that quietly transfer back into careers. Photography teaches attention to detail, team sports teach cooperation, and building models teaches patience. Third, hobbies connect people across the usual social walls, because a chess club or hiking group mixes ages and professions that would never otherwise meet. Finally, hobbies give identity a second leg to stand on. People who are only their job can collapse when the job disappears, while people with rich interests bend without breaking. A life without hobbies is efficient but fragile.
interactive.modelFollowUp: I would tell them about my former manager, the busiest person I have ever met, who kept a thirty-minute sketching habit every single lunch break. She treated it like a meeting with herself that could not be canceled. Her argument was simple: the sketching did not steal energy from her work, it returned energy to it, and her afternoons were sharper than anyone else's. Busyness is usually a scheduling problem, not a time problem, and thirty protected minutes is enough.

interactive.prompt: Some companies are testing a four-day work week with no reduction in salary. Do you think this is a good idea? Why or why not?
interactive.followUp: Which type of job or industry do you think would struggle most with a four-day week, and what alternative benefit could those workers receive instead?
interactive.model: I believe the four-day work week is a good idea for many industries, and the evidence increasingly supports it. Large trials in several countries have found that most companies kept the same output in four days, because employees cut wasted time, shortened meetings, and arrived more rested. The extra free day reduces burnout and gives workers time for family, health, and errands, which in turn lowers sick days and staff turnover. Companies that adopt it also gain a hiring advantage, since talented people compete to join them. That said, the policy is not universal. Hospitals, restaurants, and factories that must operate continuously cannot simply close on Fridays; they need rotating schedules and possibly more staff, which raises costs. My conclusion is that the four-day week should spread wherever output is measured in results rather than hours. For knowledge work especially, the five-day week is tradition, not necessity.
interactive.modelFollowUp: Healthcare would struggle most, because patients need care seven days a week and there is already a global shortage of nurses; compressing schedules could simply exhaust the remaining staff. A fair alternative would be additional paid leave banked as full recovery weeks, plus a schedule guarantee such as never working more than three consecutive night shifts. These target the same underlying goal as the four-day week, genuine rest and predictability, while respecting the reality of round-the-clock care.

interactive.prompt: When a book is made into a movie, some people prefer to read the book, while others would rather watch the film. Which do you prefer, and why?
interactive.followUp: Name a specific advantage that films have over books that you personally value, and describe a moment or scene type where that advantage really matters.
interactive.model: I almost always prefer the book, for reasons that have less to do with snobbery than with how imagination works. A novel hands me the blueprint of a world but lets me do the construction: the hero's face, the sound of the storm, and the weight of every silence are partly my own creations, which makes the story personal in a way no two readers share. Books also have room that films lack. A two-hour movie must amputate subplots and inner thoughts, while a novel can spend thirty pages inside a character's doubt, and that interior access is exactly what I read for. Finally, reading operates at my speed; I can linger over a beautiful sentence or race through a chase. I still enjoy adaptations, and occasionally a film improves on its source. But given the choice, I take the version where my imagination is a co-author.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The advantage I genuinely envy is music. A book can describe a farewell, but a film can score it, and the right melody reaches emotions faster than any paragraph. The scene type where this matters most is the wordless goodbye: two characters parting at a station, no dialogue, just faces and a rising theme. Prose has to explain that moment, while film simply plays it, and I have been moved to tears by scenes whose written versions left me dry.

interactive.prompt: Some people believe everyone should learn a second language, while others think it is unnecessary now that translation apps exist. What is your opinion?
interactive.followUp: Think about how languages are usually taught in schools. What is one weakness of that approach, and how would you fix it?
interactive.model: I believe learning a second language is still valuable, even though translation apps keep improving. The obvious benefit is practical: apps work well for reading menus, but real conversations move too fast for a phone, and business or friendship conducted through a screen translator always feels distant. The deeper benefits, however, have nothing to do with communication. Learning a language rewires how you think; you discover that some ideas exist comfortably in one language and simply have no home in another, which makes you humbler about your own worldview. Research also suggests that managing two languages strengthens memory and attention throughout life. Finally, the effort itself sends a message. When you speak to people in their own language, even badly, you show a respect that no app can deliver on your behalf. Translation software removes the necessity of language learning, but not the value of it.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The biggest weakness of school language teaching is that it treats language as a subject to be memorized rather than a skill to be used. Students spend years labeling grammar structures and still panic when a real person asks them a simple question. I would fix this by making speaking the center of every lesson from day one, even with tiny vocabulary. Ten minutes of clumsy real conversation teaches more than an hour of exercises about the past tense.

interactive.prompt: Cheap, fast-changing clothing brands are extremely popular around the world. Do you think fast fashion is a problem, or is it simply giving people what they want?
interactive.followUp: Some people say individual shopping choices change nothing and only government rules can fix fast fashion. Do you agree? What rule would help most?
interactive.model: I think fast fashion is a genuine problem, even though I understand its appeal. Cheap, trendy clothing lets people on small budgets enjoy fashion, and it would be unfair to blame consumers for wanting that. However, the hidden costs are enormous. The industry is one of the largest polluters in the world, producing mountains of textile waste because garments are designed to be worn a few times and discarded. The low prices are also possible only because workers in poor countries sew for extremely low wages, sometimes in unsafe factories. Perhaps the deepest problem is psychological: fast fashion trains us to see clothing as disposable, which quietly changes how we value objects in general. I do not expect everyone to buy expensive designer clothes instead. But buying fewer, better pieces and wearing them for years would serve both our wallets and the planet.
interactive.modelFollowUp: I partly agree: individual choices matter, but they are too slow and too rare to fix an industry this large. The most effective rule would be making brands legally responsible for their own waste, so a company that sells a million cheap shirts must pay to collect and recycle them. This would quietly raise the price of disposable clothing until durable design became the profitable option, changing the market without banning anything.

interactive.prompt: Governments spend billions of dollars on space exploration. Should this money be spent on problems here on Earth instead? Explain your view.
interactive.followUp: Private companies are now flying rockets alongside government agencies. Is it better for space exploration to be led by governments or by businesses, and why?
interactive.model: I believe governments should continue funding space exploration, though the amounts deserve honest debate. The argument that the money should solve problems on Earth sounds strong, but it hides two mistakes. First, space budgets are tiny compared with what governments spend elsewhere; canceling them entirely would barely dent poverty or disease. Second, the money is not launched into space; it pays the salaries of engineers and produces technology that flows back into ordinary life, from weather satellites that save harvests to medical imaging that began as space research. Exploration also does something less measurable: it gives societies a shared, peaceful ambition and inspires children into science careers. That said, I would prioritize projects with clear scientific return, such as climate-monitoring satellites, over expensive prestige missions. Space and Earth are not competing causes; understanding one has repeatedly protected the other.
interactive.modelFollowUp: I think the healthiest model is a partnership with governments in the lead. Companies are excellent at cutting costs and moving quickly, as reusable rockets proved, but they must chase profit, so they naturally focus on tourism and satellites. Only governments can fund pure science, like space telescopes and planetary missions, which may pay off in fifty years or never. Let businesses handle the transport while public agencies choose the destinations.

interactive.prompt: Many businesses no longer accept cash, and some countries are moving toward fully digital payments. Do you think a completely cashless society is a good idea? Why or why not?
interactive.followUp: Describe one specific group of people who would suffer most in a cashless society, and explain what could be done to protect them.
interactive.model: A fully cashless society is convenient but risky, so I oppose eliminating cash completely. Digital payments deserve their popularity: they are fast, clean, and traceable, and nobody misses counting coins at a busy register. For governments, electronic money also makes tax evasion harder. However, going completely cashless creates serious problems. Every purchase becomes a record held by banks and companies, so financial privacy essentially disappears. The system also depends entirely on electricity and networks; a long power cut or a cyber attack could freeze an entire economy within hours. Most importantly, cash is the payment method of the vulnerable: elderly people, children, and anyone without a bank account rely on it daily. My position is that digital payments should dominate, as they already do, but cash must remain legal and accepted everywhere, like a lifeboat that nobody hopes to use.
interactive.modelFollowUp: Elderly people would suffer most, because many never learned digital banking and some physically struggle with small screens and passwords. For them, losing cash means losing independence; suddenly they need help for every purchase. Protection should work in two directions: banks should be required to keep simple, human-served accounts and local branches open, while community centers offer patient, free training. The goal is giving older people a choice, not forcing them onto technology overnight.

interactive.prompt: Some countries require young people to complete a year of national service, either in the military or in community work. Do you agree with this requirement?
interactive.followUp: Some talented young people, such as athletes or musicians, say a mandatory service year would destroy their careers at a critical moment. How should the system handle such cases?
interactive.model: I support a year of mandatory national service, provided young people can choose between military and civilian options. The strongest argument is social mixing. Modern societies are increasingly divided by income, education, and politics, and national service is one of the last institutions that puts the children of doctors and the children of farmers side by side, working toward a shared goal. Participants consistently report that these friendships changed how they see their own country. The service year also delivers practical value: hospitals, elderly care, disaster response, and environmental projects all need hands, and young people gain skills, discipline, and a clearer idea of what to study afterward. Critics call it a stolen year, but I see it as an invested one. A gap between school and career, spent being useful to others, produces better citizens and often better students too.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The system should bend without breaking. Exceptional cases deserve flexible formats, not exemptions: an athlete could serve by coaching children in their sport for the same total hours spread across several years, and a musician could perform and teach in schools and hospitals. This keeps the principle that everyone serves while letting service take the shape of each talent. Complete exemptions would be fatal, because every wealthy family would suddenly discover a talented child.

interactive.prompt: Tourism brings money to popular destinations, but it also brings crowds, noise, and rising prices. Do the benefits of mass tourism outweigh its costs for local communities?
interactive.followUp: Imagine you are the mayor of a small historic town that suddenly becomes famous online. What two measures would you introduce first, and why?
interactive.model: For many communities, I believe the costs of mass tourism have started to outweigh its benefits, though the balance differs from place to place. The benefits are real: tourism creates jobs, funds the restoration of historic buildings, and lets small regions earn money from their beauty rather than their factories. However, beyond a certain volume, tourism begins consuming the very place it came to see. Housing turns into short-term rentals, pushing local families out of their own neighborhoods. Shops that once sold bread and hardware become souvenir stands, so daily life gets harder for residents. Streets, water systems, and fragile historic sites wear out under crowds they were never built for. The problem is not tourism itself but its concentration in a few famous places during a few months. Managed well, with visitor limits and taxes that fund local life, tourism is a gift; unmanaged, it becomes a slow eviction.
interactive.modelFollowUp: My first measure would be regulating short-term rentals immediately, before investors buy up the housing, because once residents leave, the town becomes a stage set and no later policy brings them back. My second would be a modest visitor fee dedicated to visible local benefits like maintaining streets and funding the school. Residents tolerate crowds far better when tourism obviously pays for their daily life rather than only for someone's profits.

interactive.prompt: More and more people shop online instead of in physical stores. Which do you prefer, and what do you think will happen to traditional shops in the future?
interactive.followUp: Describe one thing that could make online shopping worse for society in the long run, and suggest how this harm could be reduced.
interactive.model: I prefer online shopping for most purchases, though I think physical stores will survive by changing their role. Online shopping wins on the practical measures: the selection is unlimited, prices are easy to compare, reviews warn you about bad products, and everything arrives at your door. For busy people, reclaiming those hours matters enormously. However, I still visit physical shops for anything I need to touch, such as shoes and furniture, and for the small pleasure of browsing without a search box, where you find things you were not looking for. As for the future, I do not believe traditional shops will disappear, but they will become fewer and more experiential. Stores that merely display boxes will lose to the internet, while those offering expertise, service, and atmosphere, like good bookshops and bike shops, will keep their loyal customers. Retail will shrink, but it will also sharpen.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The clearest long-term harm is what empty shops do to towns. When storefronts close, streets lose their life, people stop walking, and the informal meeting places that hold a community together quietly vanish. This harm could be reduced by taxing online retailers more fairly, since they currently avoid many of the local taxes shops pay, and by letting empty storefronts convert cheaply into cafes, workshops, and services that the internet cannot deliver.

interactive.prompt: Some landlords and apartment buildings ban pets completely. Do you think people living in apartments should be allowed to keep pets such as dogs and cats?
interactive.followUp: A neighbor in your building argues that dogs have no quality of life in small apartments and that keeping them there is unfair to the animals. How would you respond?
interactive.model: I believe apartment residents should be allowed to keep pets, with reasonable rules rather than blanket bans. Pets are not a luxury hobby; for millions of people living alone, especially the elderly, a dog or cat is the difference between a lonely apartment and a home. Research consistently connects pet ownership with lower stress and more daily exercise, and dogs in particular pull their owners out into the neighborhood, creating the small social contacts that cities badly need. Of course, the concerns behind bans are not imaginary: barking disturbs neighbors, some owners never clean up, and allergies are real. But these are problems of specific owners, not of pets in general, and banning everyone for the sins of a few is lazy policy. Buildings should instead set enforceable rules on noise, cleanliness, and size, and hold the humans accountable. We should regulate bad behavior, not companionship.
interactive.modelFollowUp: I would tell the neighbor that a dog's quality of life is measured in attention and exercise, not in square meters. A dog walked twice a day and included in family life is happier in a small flat than a lonely dog pacing a big garden. The apartment is only where a dog sleeps; its real life happens on walks and in the company of its people. Size matters far less than commitment, and bad owners exist in houses too.

interactive.prompt: Some schools have stopped giving homework completely. Do you think abolishing homework is a good idea for primary school children? Explain your reasons.
interactive.followUp: Some parents worry that without homework they will have no idea what their children are learning at school. How could schools solve this problem?
interactive.model: For primary school children, I support abolishing traditional homework, and the research is largely on my side. Studies repeatedly find that homework produces little or no academic benefit before secondary school; young children learn best through instruction, play, and rest, not worksheets at the kitchen table. Meanwhile, the costs are visible in millions of homes every evening: tired children crying over exercises, parents effectively becoming unpaid teachers, and family time dissolving into supervision. Homework also deepens inequality, because a child with educated parents and a quiet desk completes it easily, while a child sharing a noisy room falls further behind through no fault of their own. What I would keep is daily reading for pleasure and the occasional creative project, like observing the moon or interviewing a grandparent. Childhood evenings should build curiosity and rest, because those, not repetition, are the real fuel of learning.
interactive.modelFollowUp: Schools can keep parents informed without using children as messengers. A short weekly note from the teacher, describing what the class studied and suggesting optional dinner-table questions, tells parents more than a pile of worksheets ever did. Occasional open classroom days let parents watch learning happen directly. Homework was always a poor communication channel anyway, because parents saw the exercises but not the understanding. Direct, simple reporting from teachers serves everyone better.

interactive.prompt: Some economists propose a universal basic income, where the government pays every citizen a fixed amount of money each month regardless of whether they work. Is this a good idea?
interactive.followUp: If a basic income were introduced in your country, what is one way society might change after ten years that most people do not expect?
interactive.model: Universal basic income is an idea I cautiously support, because the problems it targets are real even if the policy is unproven. The strongest argument is simplicity and dignity. Current welfare systems are mazes of forms and conditions that miss many people who need help and humiliate many who receive it. A universal payment eliminates the bureaucracy and removes the trap where taking a job means losing benefits, so working always pays. It would also quietly reward valuable unpaid work, such as caring for children and elderly relatives, that current economies ignore completely. The honest concerns are cost and motivation: paying every citizen is enormously expensive, and critics fear people would stop working. Trials so far suggest work reduction is small and mostly involves students and new parents, which hardly seems like a disaster. I would support starting with generous regional pilots rather than a national leap.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The unexpected change might be a quiet revolution in what jobs must pay. Today, unpleasant and essential work, like cleaning and harvest labor, is often the worst paid, because workers have no power to refuse it. With a basic income as a floor, nobody would be desperate enough to accept terrible conditions, so wages for hard, necessary jobs would rise until they matched their true importance. The salary ladder might slowly turn upside down.

interactive.prompt: Many cities have banned single-use plastic items such as bags and straws. Do you support these bans? Explain your reasons.
interactive.followUp: Some people argue that plastic bans unfairly burden ordinary consumers while big industrial polluters continue as before. How would you answer this criticism?
interactive.model: I support bans on single-use plastics, though I see them as a first step rather than a solution. The case for banning is straightforward: items like bags and straws are used for minutes and then persist for centuries, breaking into microplastics that now appear in rivers, fish, and even human blood. Voluntary approaches were tried for decades and failed, because a free plastic bag will always win against good intentions at the checkout. Bans work by changing the default: within months of my city's bag ban, carrying a cloth bag became normal and unremarkable. The honest criticism is that consumer plastics are a small share of total plastic waste, and some replacements, like thick paper bags used once, can be worse for the climate. That is why bans must be paired with reuse systems and rules for industry. Still, banning the most pointless plastics is the easiest possible beginning.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The criticism is half right, and the answer is to do both, not neither. Consumer bans matter less for their direct impact than for building the public habit and political permission for bigger steps; a society that accepted bag bans will accept packaging rules for industry. But governments must actually take those bigger steps, taxing virgin plastic production and making manufacturers pay for their packaging waste. If policy stops at straws, the critics will be proven right.

interactive.prompt: Celebrities have enormous influence on young people today. Do you think this influence is mostly positive or mostly negative? Explain your view.
interactive.followUp: Do you think parents and schools can realistically reduce the negative influence of celebrity culture? Describe one approach that might actually work with teenagers.
interactive.model: On balance, I think celebrity influence on young people is more negative than positive, though it has bright spots. The positive cases are real: athletes who model discipline, artists who speak openly about mental health, and stars who direct millions of followers toward charity. However, the machine around celebrities pushes in a harmful direction. Fame today is often disconnected from achievement; many celebrities are famous mainly for being visible, which teaches young people that attention itself is the goal. The lifestyles displayed are unreachable and heavily edited, quietly recalibrating what a normal body, home, or holiday looks like and leaving followers dissatisfied with real life. Worst is the influence on ambition: surveys find many children now name influencer as their dream job, not because they love creating things, but because it looks like reward without labor. Admiration is natural; the problem is who the system selects for it.
interactive.modelFollowUp: Forbidding or mocking celebrity culture fails with teenagers; it only makes it more attractive. What works better is teaching them to see the machinery. A class where students plan a fake influencer campaign, choosing camera angles, editing photos, and inventing a lifestyle, teaches media skepticism from the inside. Once a teenager has manufactured fake perfection with their own hands, they never look at a celebrity feed quite the same way again.

interactive.prompt: Some readers have switched completely to e-books, while others insist on paper books. Which do you prefer, and why?
interactive.followUp: Physical bookshops are struggling in many countries. Does it matter if they disappear, or is this just normal progress? Explain your view.
interactive.model: I prefer paper books for pleasure and e-books for everything else, which I suspect is where many readers eventually land. The e-book advantages are undeniable: an entire library in a coat pocket, instant delivery, adjustable text for tired eyes, and a dictionary one touch away, which transformed my reading in foreign languages. For travel and commuting, paper simply cannot compete. Yet for deep, slow reading, paper keeps winning for me. A physical book has geography; I remember arguments by where they live on the page, and the growing stack of finished pages is a quiet motivator that a percentage counter never matches. Paper also protects attention, because a book cannot ping. And there is the shelf itself, a visible autobiography that guests can browse. E-books are the better tool; paper books are the better experience, and I expect the two formats to keep sharing my life.
interactive.modelFollowUp: It matters, because a bookshop is a discovery engine, not just a warehouse. Online stores show you more of what you already like, while a good bookshop ambushes you with subjects you never searched for, guided by a human who has actually read them. Bookshops also serve as small cultural centers, hosting readings and gathering local readers. Losing them would not stop book sales, but it would quietly narrow what people ever find.

interactive.prompt: Some people do their best work early in the morning, while others are most productive late at night. Which type are you, and do you think schools and workplaces should adapt to both types?
interactive.followUp: Describe one practical problem a company might face if every employee chose their own working hours, and suggest how to manage it.
interactive.model: I am firmly a morning person, and yes, I believe institutions should adapt to both types. My best thinking happens between six and ten in the morning; by evening I can execute routine tasks but produce nothing creative. Friends of mine are my exact mirror, coming alive at ten at night. Science increasingly confirms these are biological settings, largely written in our genes, not moral choices; night owls are not lazy, and early risers are not virtuous, despite what proverbs claim. Yet schools and offices are built entirely around the morning schedule, effectively punishing a third of the population every day. Teenagers suffer most, since adolescence biologically shifts sleep later while schools start earliest. The solutions are not radical: later school start times, core office hours from ten to three with flexible edges, and judging workers by results rather than arrival time. Chronotype diversity is real diversity.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The obvious problem is collaboration: if one engineer works at dawn and another at midnight, questions wait hours for answers and meetings become impossible to schedule. The proven fix is a daily overlap window, perhaps eleven to three, when everyone must be reachable and all meetings happen. Outside that window, people work whenever their brains are best. Teams using this model report that meetings shrink and deep work grows, which is a double win.

interactive.prompt: Some people believe zoos protect endangered animals and educate the public, while others think keeping animals in enclosures is cruel. What is your view?
interactive.followUp: Some people say virtual reality could soon replace zoos, letting people experience animals without keeping any in captivity. Do you think this would work?
interactive.model: My view is that good zoos are justified, but the standard that makes a zoo good is high, and many fail it. The honest case against zoos is strong: intelligent animals like elephants and big cats show clear distress in small enclosures, and no cage recreates a hundred square kilometers of range. If zoos were only entertainment, that suffering would be indefensible. But the best modern zoos have become something else: breeding arks for species with no safe habitat left, funders of wild conservation, and the one place a city child ever stands close enough to a living rhino to care what happens to rhinos. That emotional encounter creates conservationists in a way documentaries do not. My conclusion is conditional. Zoos that prioritize spacious habitats, breeding programs, and education deserve support; roadside attractions displaying bored animals for tickets deserve closure. The label zoo covers both, and we should stop judging them together.
interactive.modelFollowUp: Virtual reality will be a wonderful supplement and a poor replacement. A headset can show you an elephant, but it cannot make the floor tremble or let you smell the animal, and children always know the difference between an image and a presence. More practically, VR generates no funding for breeding programs or habitat protection, which is the real conservation work zoos support. I would use VR to replace the worst zoos, not the best ones.

interactive.prompt: Many schools require students to wear uniforms. Do you think school uniforms are a good idea? Why or why not?
interactive.followUp: If uniforms are good for equality in schools, should adult workplaces also introduce them? Explain why or why not.
interactive.model: I lean in favor of school uniforms, mainly for reasons of equality, though I want them designed sensibly. The strongest argument is what uniforms erase: the daily fashion competition in which children are ranked by their parents' income. In schools without uniforms, clothing becomes a visible class system, and children are cruel about it; a uniform makes the poorest and richest student look the same at the school gate. Uniforms also simplify mornings and reportedly reduce conflicts over dress codes, freeing teachers from policing outfits. The counterarguments deserve respect: uniforms restrict self-expression, and forcing identical clothing can feel militaristic. But children have evenings, weekends, and countless other channels for expressing identity; relying on branded clothes for identity is exactly the habit schools should not encourage. My conditions are that uniforms be comfortable, weather-appropriate, and cheap, with subsidies available, so the equality argument is not undermined by the price tag.
interactive.modelFollowUp: I would not extend uniforms to most adult workplaces, because the situations differ fundamentally. Children are trapped in a daily status competition they did not choose and cannot escape, so schools protect them by leveling it. Adults have finished forming their identities, chose their workplace, and can generally afford acceptable clothes. Where function demands it, like hospitals and airlines, uniforms make sense. Elsewhere, a dress code is enough; adults deserve the small freedom.

interactive.prompt: Artificial intelligence tools can now write text, create images, and answer questions. Do you think these tools will mostly help workers or mostly replace them?
interactive.followUp: Some schools ban AI writing tools, while others teach students to use them. Which approach do you think prepares students better, and why?
interactive.model: I believe AI tools will mostly help workers, but through a hard transition that policy must soften. In my own experience, these tools behave like tireless junior assistants: they draft the routine email, summarize the long document, and produce a first version of almost anything, while judgment, taste, and responsibility stay human. History supports some optimism here, since spreadsheets did not eliminate accountants; they eliminated arithmetic and multiplied analysis. The realistic worry is not mass unemployment but redistribution: workers whose entire job was the routine layer, such as basic translation, template writing, or simple illustration, face real displacement, and telling them new jobs will appear eventually is cold comfort. The gap between workers who master these tools and those who ignore them will also widen quickly. So my answer is conditional. AI augments those who adapt and threatens those who cannot, which makes retraining and honest transition support the real battleground, not the technology.
interactive.modelFollowUp: Teaching beats banning, for one practical reason: these tools will sit on every desk of every job these students ever hold, so graduating without mastering them means graduating unprepared. Banning also fails technically, pushing use underground where nobody learns judgment. The skill schools should teach is critical collaboration: when to trust the tool, how to verify its claims, and how to improve its drafts. That said, foundational writing must still be learned unassisted, just as mathematicians still learn arithmetic.

interactive.prompt: Some students take a gap year between high school and university to work or travel. Is this a good use of a year, or a waste of time?
interactive.followUp: Gap years are often only possible for students from wealthier families. How could the opportunity be made available to everyone?
interactive.model: I think a well-planned gap year is one of the best investments a student can make, and a drifting one is genuinely wasted time; the year itself is neutral. The case in favor starts with a quiet fact: eighteen-year-olds choose university programs knowing almost nothing about work or themselves, and many discover the mistake three expensive years later. A year of working, volunteering, or traveling supplies exactly the missing data. Students who arrive after a gap year tend to be noticeably more motivated, because studying is now their own decision rather than the conveyor belt's next station. Employers also quietly value the maturity. The risks are real but manageable: momentum can be lost, and an unstructured year on a sofa builds nothing. The difference is a plan with commitments, a job, a course, a project, made before the year begins. Used deliberately, the gap year turns education from a default into a choice.
interactive.modelFollowUp: The fairest solution is turning the gap year into a paid national program. If governments offered a year of service in hospitals, schools, and environmental projects, with a modest salary and housing support, the gap year would stop being a privilege of families who can fund travel. Employers and universities formally recognizing the program would complete the change. A poorer student would then gain the same self-knowledge and maturity while earning rather than spending.

samples.prompt: Describe a trip or journey that you will never forget. Where did you go, and what made it so memorable?
samples.model: The journey I will never forget is a night train I took through the mountains with my father when I was sixteen. The trip itself was ordinary in purpose, a visit to relatives, but everything about it became extraordinary. Our train left at midnight, and instead of sleeping we sat in the corridor by a half-open window, drinking tea from paper cups while dark hills slid past. My father, who rarely talked about himself, began telling stories from his own teenage years: his first job, a friend he had lost, the night he decided to leave his village. I understood him more in those six hours than in the previous sixteen years. At dawn the train crossed a long bridge above a river full of fog, and we both went quiet at the same moment. Nothing dramatic happened on that trip, and that is exactly the point. It taught me that the memorable part of travel is rarely the destination; it is who you become while moving, and who happens to be sitting beside you.

samples.prompt: Describe a person who has had a strong influence on your life. Who are they, and how did they influence you?
samples.model: The person who influenced me most was my secondary school mathematics teacher, Mr. Halim. Before his class, I believed intelligence was something you either had or did not have, and I had quietly decided I did not. Mr. Halim attacked that belief with a strange method: he celebrated mistakes. When a student solved a problem incorrectly on the board, he would say, with real enthusiasm, that we had just discovered a very useful wrong path, and he would trace exactly where the reasoning slipped. Errors stopped being humiliations and became information. Under that system I improved rapidly, but the mathematics was the smaller gift. The larger one was the idea that ability grows with honest effort and that being wrong in public is survivable, even valuable. I still hear his voice when I start something difficult, whether it is a new language or a job interview. He retired years ago, and I doubt he remembers me, but his way of thinking now runs quietly inside my own, which may be the deepest influence one person can have on another.

samples.prompt: Describe a difficult decision you had to make. What were your options, and how did you finally choose?
samples.model: The most difficult decision of my life so far was choosing between a safe job offer in my hometown and an uncertain opportunity in another city. The hometown job paid decently, sat ten minutes from my parents' house, and promised a calm, predictable career. The distant opportunity paid slightly less, involved a field I barely knew, and required leaving everyone I loved. For weeks I made lists of advantages and disadvantages, and the lists always favored staying, yet something in me refused to accept the result. The turning point came from a simple question a friend asked: which choice would I regret at seventy? Suddenly the decision reorganized itself. Staying was a decision I could make again at any point in my life, but the door to leaving would not stay open forever. I took the uncertain job, struggled badly for the first six months, and then slowly built a life I could never have imagined at home. I learned that difficult decisions are rarely between good and bad; they are between two different people you might become.

samples.prompt: Describe a skill you taught yourself. How did you learn it, and what did the process teach you?
samples.model: Two years ago I taught myself to touch-type, a skill I had avoided for my entire life while proudly hunting keys with two fingers. The trigger was embarrassment: during a video call, colleagues could hear my slow, loud typing as I took notes. I found a free website, committed to fifteen minutes every morning before breakfast, and forced myself to stop looking at the keyboard completely, which felt like learning to walk again. For the first two weeks I was dramatically worse than before, typing at half my old speed, and quitting was tempting every single day. Around week four something shifted; my fingers began finding letters before my mind finished asking. Within three months I typed twice as fast as I ever had, without thought. The deeper lesson had nothing to do with keyboards. I learned that improvement often begins with a period of getting worse, and that most people quit inside that valley. Now, whenever a new skill makes me clumsy and frustrated, I recognize the valley and keep walking, because I finally know what is on the other side.

samples.prompt: Describe a place where you feel completely relaxed. What is it like, and why does it have this effect on you?
samples.model: The place where I feel completely relaxed is a small stone breakwater at the edge of my grandmother's coastal town. It is nothing a tourist would photograph: a rough arm of grey rocks reaching into the sea, with a rusted green lamp at the far end. I have been walking to that lamp since I was a child, and the ritual never changes. You climb over the uneven stones, find the flat one third from the end, and sit facing open water. The effect comes from a combination of things. The sound of waves is loud enough to cover every other noise, including the noise inside my head. The horizon is completely empty, which somehow empties me too. And because no one else bothers to walk out there, the place carries no social pressure at all; nobody is watching, so there is no one to perform for. I have made every major decision of my adult life on that rock. Cities have parks and spas, but true relaxation, for me, requires salt wind, familiar stones, and the patience of the sea.

samples.prompt: Describe a time when you helped someone. What did you do, and how did the experience affect you?
samples.model: In my second year of university, I noticed a first-year student from abroad sitting alone in the cafeteria every single day, always with a textbook he never seemed to read. One afternoon I sat across from him and asked about his course. His English was hesitant and he apologized constantly for it, which told me everything about his semester so far. We started meeting twice a week, officially so I could help with his coursework, but really so he could practice conversation without fear. I introduced him to my friends, taught him the local slang that no textbook includes, and dragged him to a football game he pretended to understand. By spring he was arguing about films in rapid English and correcting my grammar for revenge. The effect on me was unexpected and lasting. I had assumed helping someone was a donation, energy flowing one way, but I gained as much as he did: a close friend, a window into another culture, and the discovery that loneliness in a crowd is common and curable. Since then I look for the person sitting alone.

samples.prompt: Describe an important goal you have for the future. Why does it matter to you, and how do you plan to achieve it?
samples.model: My most important goal for the next five years is to become fluent enough in English to work internationally, and this test is literally part of the plan. The goal matters for practical reasons, since the interesting jobs and research in my field increasingly happen in English, but the deeper motivation is personal. I grew up watching my mother, a brilliant engineer, stay silent in international meetings because her ideas could not cross the language barrier fast enough, and I watched less capable people receive credit for thoughts she had first. I decided early that language would never be my ceiling. My plan is deliberately unglamorous: one hour of focused study every morning, one film or series episode without subtitles every evening, and a weekly conversation exchange with a partner who wants to learn my language. I also keep a notebook of every phrase that surprises me. Fluency, as I understand it now, is not a mountain you climb once but a garden you water daily. I intend to keep watering until the silence my mother experienced is impossible for me.

samples.prompt: Describe a tradition in your family or your culture that is important to you. What happens, and why do you value it?
samples.model: The tradition I value most is my family's first-morning walk. Every year, on the first day of the new year, my entire extended family walks the same five-kilometer route through the fields outside my grandparents' village, regardless of weather. The rule, established by my great-grandfather, is beautifully simple: everyone walks, everyone talks, and no one mentions work or money. Cousins I see once a year fall into step beside me and, somewhere around the second kilometer, real conversation begins, the kind that never happens across a dinner table. Marriages have been announced on that path, arguments quietly repaired, and grief shared in the space between hedgerows. I value the tradition because it is engineered honesty. Walking side by side, without eye contact, people say things they could never say face to face, and the moving landscape absorbs whatever is difficult. As family members spread across different cities and countries, that one shared hour of mud and cold air has become the thread that proves we are still a single fabric. I will walk it with my own children someday.

samples.prompt: Describe a challenge or problem you faced and overcame. What was the situation, and what did you learn from it?
samples.model: The hardest challenge I have overcome was my fear of public speaking, which in university went beyond ordinary nervousness. During my first class presentation, my mind emptied completely; I stood in silence for what felt like an hour, then sat down without finishing. The humiliation was so sharp that I began choosing courses by whether they required presentations. The turning point came when I realized my avoidance was quietly deciding my future for me. I attacked the problem in embarrassingly small steps: first asking one question per lecture in a large hall, then presenting to two friends, then five, then volunteering for a low-stakes talk at a student club. I also changed my preparation, memorizing only my opening minute and my closing minute, which removed the terror of a blank start. Two years later I presented my final project to a full auditorium, imperfectly but calmly. The lesson I carry is that courage is not a personality trait; it is a staircase, and the steps can be as small as you need, as long as you keep climbing them in order.

samples.prompt: Describe how a piece of technology has changed your daily life. What did life look like before and after?
samples.model: The technology that most changed my daily life is not a phone or a laptop but a simple electronic bicycle. Before I bought it, my commute defined my mood: forty minutes each way in crowded buses, pressed against strangers, arriving at work already tired and arriving home already irritated. I had tried a normal bicycle, but the hills on my route defeated me in every summer shirt I owned. The electric motor changed the equation entirely. The same journey became twenty-five minutes of open air, fast enough to beat the bus and gentle enough that I arrive without sweating. The changes rippled outward in ways I never predicted. I began noticing seasons again, the exact week blossoms appear along the canal. I discovered shops and shortcuts invisible from a bus window. My monthly transport costs collapsed, and a persistent back problem, caused by years of standing in buses, quietly disappeared. Most surprisingly, the ride home now works as a boundary between work and family, so my evenings belong to my evenings. One machine returned two hours of my day to me.

samples.prompt: Describe a mistake you made and what you learned from it. What happened, and how did it change you?
samples.model: The mistake I learned most from was staying silent in my first job when I noticed a serious error in a project. I was three months into the position, and during a review I spotted that our cost calculations used an outdated price list. Everyone senior seemed confident, so I convinced myself I must be misunderstanding something and said nothing. Three weeks later the client discovered the discrepancy, the project lost money, and the team spent a painful month rebuilding trust. When I finally admitted I had suspected the problem, my manager was not angry about the error itself; she was disappointed that I had priced my own comfort above the team's success. That sentence rearranged me. The lesson was not simply to speak up, but to understand what silence actually costs and who pays for it. Since then, I follow a personal rule: if a doubt survives one night of thinking, I voice it the next morning, politely and with evidence. I have been wrong many times since, but never silently wrong, and the difference matters.

samples.prompt: Describe a happy memory from your childhood. What happened, and why do you think you remember it so clearly?
samples.model: My clearest happy memory is of the summer blackout when I was eight. A storm knocked out electricity in our entire district for a whole evening, and my ordinary parents became suddenly magical. My father lit candles and made shadow animals on the wall, and my mother, who never had time for anything, sat on the kitchen floor with us and told stories about her own childhood while we ate bread and jam, because nothing could be cooked. The neighbors came down to the courtyard, someone brought a guitar, and the adults sang badly and laughed about it. I remember lying on a blanket outside, seeing more stars than I knew existed, because the city lights were off too. I think the memory stays so vivid because it was the first time I saw the adults in my life stop performing adulthood. Nothing was scheduled, nothing was productive, and everyone was completely present. Much of what I now believe about happiness, that it is attention rather than circumstances, traces straight back to that powerless, glowing evening.

samples.prompt: Describe your ideal job. What would you do every day, and why would this work suit you?
samples.model: My ideal job would be designing and teaching short, practical courses for adults, a mix of teacher, writer, and organizer. My days would divide into two halves I both love. Mornings would be spent building materials: distilling a complicated subject into exercises that let people discover ideas themselves, which is a puzzle I find endlessly satisfying. Afternoons would be for the classroom, watching a group of adults who arrived tired from work slowly light up as something confusing becomes clear. The job suits me for specific reasons. I am energized rather than drained by explaining things; friends already use me as their unofficial translator for contracts, technology, and official forms. I also need visible impact, and teaching provides the most direct feedback loop I know: confusion walks in, understanding walks out. Finally, the job would feed my own curiosity, since teaching a subject forces you to understand it honestly. I would happily accept a modest salary for work where the product is a more capable person, because I have never once regretted time spent explaining something well.

samples.prompt: Describe a time when a stranger was unexpectedly kind to you. What happened, and how did it affect you?
samples.model: Several years ago I missed the last train home from a city where I knew nobody, with a nearly dead phone and not enough money for a hotel. I must have looked as lost as I felt, because an older woman selling flowers near the station asked me what was wrong. After hearing my situation, she did something I still find remarkable: she walked me two streets to a small guesthouse run by her cousin, negotiated a price that matched exactly the money I had, and told the cousin to give me breakfast because I looked like someone's son. In the morning, when I tried to thank her properly at the flower stand, she waved me away and said kindness is rent we pay for living among people. The effect on me was permanent and practical. I stopped believing that cities are cold; they are full of people waiting for a reason. And I started paying that rent myself: I now approach lost-looking strangers instead of walking past, and every time I do, I think of her.

samples.prompt: Describe how you met one of your closest friends. What brought you together, and why do you think the friendship has lasted?
samples.model: I met my closest friend because of a broken washing machine in our university dormitory. We were strangers standing in the laundry room at midnight, staring at the same machine that had swallowed both our coins, and the absurdity of the situation did the introductions for us. Fixing it together, badly, with a coat hanger, took an hour, and by the end we had covered families, hometowns, and our shared suspicion of the dormitory cook. What brought us together permanently, though, was discovering that we studied opposite subjects and disagreed about nearly everything enjoyable: films, music, politics, even food. Our friendship became a fifteen-year argument that neither of us wants to win. I think it has lasted for exactly that reason, plus one more: reliability in the small things. He has never once forgotten to ask about something important I mentioned, and I have learned from him that remembering is a form of love. We live in different cities now, but the argument continues weekly by phone, and I intend to keep losing it for decades.

samples.prompt: Describe a serious disagreement you had with a friend or family member and how it was resolved. What was it about, and what did you learn?
samples.model: The most serious disagreement of my adult life was with my father, about my decision to change careers. To him, leaving a stable position for an uncertain field was not a plan but a betrayal of everything his generation had worked to give mine, and he said so at full volume. For almost two months we spoke only through my mother, both of us waiting for an apology we felt owed. The resolution came from an unexpected direction. My aunt told me a story I had never heard: my father, at my exact age, had wanted to leave his job to start a workshop, and his own father had forbidden it. Suddenly his anger looked completely different; it was fear wearing anger's clothes, and grief for his own unlived choice. I visited him, and instead of defending my decision again, I asked about the workshop. We talked for four hours. He never explicitly approved my change, but he started asking careful questions about it, which in his language is a blessing. I learned that in family arguments, the stated topic is rarely the real one.

samples.prompt: Describe a festival or public event you attended that you enjoyed. What was it like, and what do you remember most about it?
samples.model: The event I remember best is a lantern festival I attended in a small lakeside town, almost by accident, while visiting a friend. Once a year, at the end of summer, the whole town writes wishes on paper lanterns and floats them onto the lake after dark. We arrived knowing nothing and were immediately adopted by a local family who insisted we take two of their lanterns, because wishes, the grandmother explained, multiply when shared with strangers. What I remember most is the moment the floating began. Thousands of small lights left the shore at once, and the crowd, which had been noisy all evening, went almost completely silent without anyone asking. You could hear water and quiet laughter and nothing else. My friend and I stood among hundreds of strangers, all watching their hopes drift toward the middle of a dark lake, and it felt like seeing the inside of a town turned outward for one hour. I have attended larger and louder festivals since, but none that made silence feel like the main performance.

samples.prompt: Describe the best piece of advice you have ever received. Who gave it to you, and how has it influenced your life?
samples.model: The best advice I ever received came from my first boss, delivered casually while she signed papers: never make a permanent decision from a temporary emotion. I was twenty-three and holding a resignation letter, furious about a colleague who had taken credit for my work. She did not argue with my anger; she agreed the situation was unfair, then asked me to keep the letter in my drawer for two weeks and hand it in afterward with her full support if I still wanted to. Twelve days later the colleague was transferred, and a project I ended up leading changed my career. The letter never left the drawer. Since then, the advice has functioned as a mandatory waiting room for my biggest choices. I have postponed angry emails, dramatic announcements, and one very tempting impulsive purchase of a motorcycle, and in nearly every case the two-week version of me was grateful to the day-one version for waiting. Emotions are honest information, she taught me, but they are terrible signatures. I have passed the advice on at least a dozen times.

samples.prompt: Describe how you deal with stress. What kinds of situations stress you most, and what methods actually work for you?
samples.model: Stress, for me, comes less from the amount of work than from fog: periods when I have many obligations but no clear picture of them, all circling my head at once like planes waiting to land. Deadlines I can see clearly rarely frighten me; invisible ones ruin my sleep. Over the years I have found three methods that genuinely work, all embarrassingly simple. The first is externalizing: when anxiety rises, I write every single worry on paper, big and small, and the relief is almost chemical, because a written list is finite while a mental one feels infinite. The second is walking, specifically without my phone; problems seem to reorganize themselves at five kilometers per hour in a way they refuse to do at a desk. The third is shrinking the next step, asking not how I will finish everything but what the next twenty minutes should contain. What never works for me is pushing through with more coffee and less sleep, which converts stress into mistakes and mistakes into more stress. Managing stress, I have learned, is mostly managing clarity.

samples.prompt: Describe your dream home. Where would it be, and what features would make it special to you?
samples.model: My dream home is not large, which surprises people, but it is very specific. It would stand at the edge of a small town, close enough to walk to a bakery, far enough that the loudest night sound is rain. The house itself would be single-story with wooden floors that creak honestly, and its heart would be a kitchen with a table big enough for eight, because my favorite version of life happens around long dinners that nobody wants to end. Three features are non-negotiable. First, a wall of bookshelves with a ladder, both library and autobiography. Second, a covered porch deep enough to sit outside during storms, since watching rain from shelter is my oldest definition of luxury. Third, a small workshop room where unfinished projects can live without apology. What my dream deliberately excludes matters too: no huge lawn demanding maintenance, no rooms reserved for guests who visit yearly, no cinema in the basement. I have noticed that my imagined house is optimized for mornings, books, and company rather than for impressing anyone, and I take that as a good sign about the person doing the dreaming.
